comments:
Summary
This paper proposes STransE, a simple embedding model for knowledge-base completion that combines ideas from Structured Embedding (SE) and TransE: each relation is modeled by two relation-specific projection matrices (one for head, one for tail) plus a translation vector, and triples are scored by the distance between the projected head plus the relation vector and the projected tail. The model is trained with a standard margin-based ranking loss using SGD and evaluated on standard link-prediction benchmarks WN18 and FB15k. The authors report improvements over SE, TransE, and several related baselines (especially among models that do not exploit path information or external corpora), and they provide some analysis by relation category.

Strengths
- The model is conceptually simple and intuitively motivated: allowing different projections for head and tail per relation addresses asymmetry and role-specific attributes.  
- Experiments follow standard protocols (raw/filtered settings, Hits@10, MRR, mean rank) and hyperparameters are tuned on validation data. The paper compares against a broad set of prior embedding methods and reports gains on the two benchmark datasets.  
- Practical details are given (initialization from TransE, identity matrices, Bernoulli negative sampling, constraints on norms), which aids reproducibility; code link is provided.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement
- Novelty is incremental: STransE is a straightforward combination of existing approaches (SE + TransE). The paper does not introduce fundamentally new modeling principles beyond combining projection matrices and translation vectors.  
- The model increases per-relation parameters substantially (two full k×k matrices per relation). The paper notes this partly (worse on 1-1 relations could be due to extra parameters) but provides limited discussion or empirical analysis of scalability, memory cost, or overfitting. A comparison of parameter counts and wall-clock training time / memory usage versus baselines would be useful.  
- Ablation analyses are limited. For instance, it would strengthen the paper to show the contribution of each component (e.g., two matrices vs one matrix vs diagonal or low-rank approximations, effect of initializing from TransE vs random) and to report sensitivity to k (embedding size) and regularization.  
- Evaluation is limited to WN18 and FB15k. While these are standard, broader evaluation (or more detailed error analysis) would increase confidence in generality; in particular, more analysis about cases where STransE helps vs fails (beyond the relation-category summary) would be informative.  
- The paper acknowledges that models using path information or external text perform better. It would be valuable to discuss how STransE could be integrated into such systems and whether the extra per-relation parameters hinder such integration.

Suggestions
- Include ablation experiments: single shared matrix vs two matrices, diagonal/low-rank matrices, and experiments without TransE initialization.  
- Report model size and training time comparisons for practitioners concerned with scalability.  
- Provide qualitative examples of predictions that STransE corrects compared to TransE/SE, to better illustrate the model’s advantages.  
- Discuss parameter-efficient variants (e.g., factorized relation matrices) as practical alternatives when the number of relations is large.

Overall assessment
The paper presents a straightforward, well-executed incremental improvement on popular embedding models for KB completion. It offers a competitive and easy-to-understand baseline that will be useful to the community. The empirical evaluation is solid within the chosen benchmarks, though additional analysis (ablation, scalability, qualitative errors) would strengthen the contribution. I recommend acceptance as a solid incremental contribution (suitable for poster presentation).
